entries.title: Tendon, Ligament, and Muscle Injury, Osteotendinous, Myotendinous, and Muscle-to-Bone Junction Therapy Perspectives with Growth Factors and Stable Gastric Pentadecapeptide BPC 157-A Review.
entries.displayTitle: BPC-157 Outperforms Growth Factors in Junctional Musculoskeletal Healing
entries.summary: This systematic review compared BPC-157 against PRP and growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β1, IGF-1, FGF, VEGF, BMPs) for tendon, ligament, muscle, and junctional injuries, primarily in rat models. BPC-157 uniquely demonstrated efficacy across all tissue types and junctions, both systemically and locally, without requiring carriers or scaffolds.
keyFindings: Growth factors improved tendon/ligament healing but showed limited or no efficacy at osteotendinous and myotendinous junctions.
BPC-157 administered systemically (IP, intragastric, drinking water) or locally (cream) consistently healed all injury types.
PDGF, TGF-β1, and IGF-1 failed in muscle lesion models despite broader tendon efficacy.
BPC-157 acted as a full-range cytoprotection mediator without scaffolds, carriers, or tissue-engineering constructs.
entries.limitations: Evidence base is predominantly preclinical rat studies; human clinical trial data for BPC-157 in musculoskeletal injuries remains absent.
entries.context: This is the most comprehensive synthesis to date comparing BPC-157 against established growth factors across the full spectrum of musculoskeletal and junctional injuries, establishing BPC-157's unique translational profile.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing animal and preclinical evidence on BPC-157 musculoskeletal mechanisms

entries.title: Emerging Use of BPC-157 in Orthopaedic Sports Medicine: A Systematic Review.
entries.displayTitle: BPC-157 in Orthopaedic Sports Medicine: Systematic Review of Evidence
entries.journal: HSS journal : the musculoskeletal journal of Hospital for Special Surgery
entries.summary: A systematic review of 544 articles (36 included, 35 preclinical, 1 clinical) synthesized BPC-157's mechanisms, musculoskeletal outcomes, metabolism, and safety. Preclinical evidence supports healing in tendon, ligament, muscle, and bone; clinical data remain extremely limited.
keyFindings: 36 studies included from 544 identified; 35 were preclinical, only 1 was clinical.
BPC-157 enhanced GH receptor expression, angiogenesis pathways, and reduced inflammatory cytokines in preclinical models.
Improved functional, structural, and biomechanical outcomes across muscle, tendon, ligament, and bone injury models.
Single retrospective clinical study: 7 of 12 patients reported knee pain relief >6 months after intraarticular injection.
entries.limitations: Virtually all included studies are preclinical; the sole clinical study is a small retrospective series of 12 patients, making evidence quality very low.
entries.context: First systematic review appraising BPC-157 specifically from an orthopaedic sports medicine perspective, providing the most comprehensive synthesis of its musculoskeletal evidence base to date despite the predominance of preclinical data.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human and animal evidence

entries.title: Stable Gastric Pentadecapeptide BPC 157 and Wound Healing.
entries.displayTitle: BPC-157 Mechanisms in Wound Healing Across Multiple Tissue Types
entries.summary: This review synthesizes animal and translational evidence on BPC-157's wound-healing effects across skin, gastrointestinal, tendon, nerve, and vascular tissues in rat models, highlighting consistent efficacy across injury types and administration routes.
keyFindings: BPC-157 accelerated healing of incisional, excisional, burn, and diabetic ulcer wounds in rats.
Peptide resolved vessel constriction, platelet plug formation, and fibrin clot stabilization.
Counteracted both arterial and venous thrombosis and improved ischemia/reperfusion outcomes.
Rapidly upregulated multiple wound-healing gene expressions in rat excision skin wounds.
entries.limitations: Narrative review without systematic search or meta-analytic methodology; conclusions rest primarily on rat models with limited human clinical validation.
entries.context: Consolidates a broad body of preclinical mechanistic work establishing BPC-157 as a multi-tissue wound-healing agent, and references prior human ulcerative colitis and multiple sclerosis trials, making it a key reference for researchers surveying BPC-157's therapeutic scope.
entries.landmarkReason: Comprehensive mechanistic review synthesizing foundational animal evidence on BPC-157's wound-healing pathways

entries.title: Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide Recovers Motor Function in SD Rats after Focal Stroke.
entries.displayTitle: Intranasal DSIP Accelerates Motor Recovery After Focal Stroke in Rats
entries.summary: Sprague-Dawley rats received intranasal DSIP (120 µg/kg) before and after middle cerebral artery occlusion. DSIP-treated animals showed significantly improved motor recovery on rotarod testing over 21 days, though infarct volume reduction was not statistically significant.
keyFindings: DSIP administered intranasally at 120 µg/kg for 8 days significantly improved rotarod motor performance post-stroke.
Brain infarction volume was smaller in DSIP-treated animals but did not reach statistical significance.
Motor coordination and bilateral asymmetry improvements were observed across multiple post-stroke timepoints.
Intranasal route demonstrated feasibility for DSIP delivery in a focal ischemia model.
entries.limitations: Small animal study with no specification of exact group sizes; infarct volume reduction did not reach significance, limiting conclusions about neuroprotection.
entries.context: This is among the few controlled studies examining DSIP's neuroprotective and neurorecovery potential in a clinically relevant stroke model, providing mechanistic and dosing rationale for intranasal peptide delivery.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study — primary evidence for DSIP in stroke motor recovery

entries.title: DSIP-Like KND Peptide Reduces Brain Infarction in C57Bl/6 and Reduces Myocardial Infarction in SD Rats When Administered during Reperfusion.
entries.displayTitle: DSIP Analogue KND Cuts Brain and Heart Infarction in Rodents
entries.summary: DSIP and its structural analogue KND were tested in rat myocardial infarction and mouse focal stroke models. KND administered at reperfusion reduced myocardial infarct area by ~55% and brain infarct volume by ~39% versus saline controls.
keyFindings: KND reduced myocardial infarction area from 42.1 ± 9.2% to 19.1 ± 7.3% vs. saline control.
Brain infarction volume dropped from 12.2 ± 5.6% to 7.4 ± 3.5% with intranasal KND.
Cardio- and neuroprotection only achieved when peptide given during reperfusion, not occlusion.
Administration during occlusion caused 100% mortality, a critical safety finding.
entries.limitations: Animal-only study with small group sizes; results may not translate to human ischemia-reperfusion injury.
entries.context: This is among the first studies directly comparing DSIP and its analogue KND in both cardiac and cerebral ischemia-reperfusion models, establishing timing-dependent protective effects and a clear safety boundary.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing cardio- and neuroprotective mechanism and critical dosing-timing constraint

entries.title: The Antioxidant Tetrapeptide Epitalon Enhances Delayed Wound Healing in an in Vitro Model of Diabetic Retinopathy.
entries.displayTitle: Epithalon Restores Wound Healing in Diabetic Retinopathy Cell Model
entries.summary: This in vitro study tested Epithalon (AEDG) on high-glucose-injured human retinal pigment epithelial cells (ARPE-19). Epithalon restored impaired wound healing by reducing ROS and inhibiting hyperglycemia-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis.
keyFindings: High glucose exposure delayed wound healing and elevated intracellular ROS in ARPE-19 cells.
Epithalon reversed high-glucose-induced suppression of antioxidant gene expression.
Epithalon inhibited EMT and downregulated fibrosis-related genes upregulated by hyperglycemia.
Findings suggest Epithalon as a candidate therapeutic for proliferative diabetic retinopathy.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to a single human cell line in vitro; no animal or clinical validation of efficacy or safety is provided.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to characterize Epithalon's antioxidant and anti-fibrotic mechanism specifically in a diabetic retinopathy model, linking its known antioxidant properties to a clinically relevant ocular complication.
entries.landmarkReason: Mechanistic study establishing primary therapeutic mechanism in diabetic retinopathy model

entries.title: Exploring the beneficial effects of GHK-Cu on an experimental model of colitis and the underlying mechanisms.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu Reduces Colitis Severity via SIRT1/STAT3 Pathway in Mice
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse, with in vitro mechanistic study
entries.summary: This study evaluated GHK-Cu in a DSS-induced murine ulcerative colitis model, combining in vivo, in vitro, network pharmacology, and molecular docking approaches. GHK-Cu reduced inflammation, improved mucosal healing, and upregulated tight junction proteins by modulating the SIRT1/STAT3 signaling pathway.
keyFindings: GHK-Cu improved disease activity index, reduced colonic edema and shortening, and increased goblet cell numbers in DSS-induced mice.
GHK-Cu suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in colon tissue.
GHK-Cu upregulated SIRT1 and suppressed p-STAT3, promoting ZO-1 and Occludin expression in a co-culture model.
siSTAT3 transfection abolished GHK-Cu's mucosal healing effect, confirming STAT3 as a core mechanistic target.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to murine and in vitro models; no human data or pharmacokinetic characterization are provided.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to systematically map GHK-Cu's mechanism of action in colitis, identifying SIRT1/STAT3 as the primary signaling axis and using siRNA validation to confirm pathway dependence.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing SIRT1/STAT3 pathway for GHK-Cu in colitis

entries.title: The glycyl-l-histidyl-l-lysine-Cutripeptide complex attenuates lung inflammation and fibrosis in silicosis by targeting peroxiredoxin 6.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu Reduces Lung Inflammation and Fibrosis in Silicosis via PRDX6
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse with in vitro mechanistic study
entries.summary: This study investigated GHK-Cu's therapeutic effects in a mouse silicosis model and RAW264.7 alveolar macrophage cell line, identifying peroxiredoxin 6 (PRDX6) as a molecular target. GHK-Cu binding to PRDX6 attenuated oxidative stress, lung inflammation, and fibrosis without significant systemic toxicity.
keyFindings: GHK-Cu directly binds to peroxiredoxin 6 (PRDX6), a key antioxidant enzyme in alveolar macrophages.
Treatment reduced silica-induced pulmonary inflammation and fibrosis in a mouse model.
GHK-Cu suppressed crystalline silica-induced oxidative stress in RAW264.7 alveolar macrophages.
No significant systemic toxicity was observed at therapeutic doses in treated mice.
entries.limitations: Study relies solely on a mouse model and a single cell line; no human pharmacokinetic or efficacy data are provided.
entries.context: Silicosis has no approved specific therapy; this study is among the first to identify a precise molecular target (PRDX6) for GHK-Cu's antioxidant and antifibrotic action, providing a mechanistic basis for potential clinical development.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study identifying PRDX6 as GHK-Cu's primary target in pulmonary fibrosis

entries.title: Glycyl-l-histidyl-l-lysine-Curescues cigarette smoking-induced skeletal muscle dysfunction via a sirtuin 1-dependent pathway.
entries.displayTitle: GHK-Cu Rescues Smoking-Induced Muscle Dysfunction via SIRT1 Pathway
entries.journal: Journal of cachexia, sarcopenia and muscle
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse + in vitro mechanistic study with human observational data
entries.summary: This study measured plasma GHK levels in COPD patients versus healthy controls and tested GHK-Cu in cigarette smoke-exposed mouse and cell models. GHK-Cu reduced muscle atrophy, improved grip strength, and activated SIRT1-dependent antioxidant and mitochondrial pathways.
keyFindings: Plasma GHK was significantly lower in COPD patients vs. controls (70.27 vs. 133.0 ng/mL, P=0.009).
GHK-Cu (2 mg/kg) increased muscle cross-sectional area by ~113% and improved grip strength by ~93% vs. smoking controls.
GHK-Cu directly bound SIRT1 (binding energy −6.1 kcal/mol), activating FoxO3a, Nrf2, and PGC-1α pathways.
In vitro, GHK-Cu restored myosin heavy chain expression and reduced atrophy markers MuRF1 and atrogin-1.
entries.limitations: Human observational component is very small (n=9 COPD, n=11 controls), limiting conclusions about clinical relevance of plasma GHK as a biomarker.
entries.context: This is the first study to identify SIRT1 as the primary mechanistic target of GHK-Cu in skeletal muscle, linking endogenous GHK deficiency in COPD to muscle wasting and establishing a therapeutic rationale for GHK-Cu supplementation.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing SIRT1 pathway as primary target of GHK-Cu in muscle

entries.title: Systematic Review of the Efficacy and Safety of Topical Glutathione in Dermatology.
entries.displayTitle: Topical Glutathione Shows Promise for Skin Lightening and Antioxidant Effects
entries.journal: The Journal of clinical and aesthetic dermatology
entries.summary: A PRISMA-guided systematic review of 5 clinical trials evaluated the safety and efficacy of topical glutathione in dermatology, finding evidence for improved hyperpigmentation, reduced UV-induced erythema, decreased melanin index, and lower TEWL, though safety data remains limited.
keyFindings: Only 5 clinical trials met inclusion criteria from 446 screened articles.
S-acyl glutathione 2% cream significantly reduced UV-induced erythema (p=0.0003).
2% oxidized glutathione lotion decreased melanin index, TEWL, and wrinkles (p<0.05–0.01).
GAP blend increased GSH/GSSG ratio, improving oxidative damage repair capacity.
entries.limitations: Only 5 clinical trials met inclusion criteria, indicating a very thin evidence base; safety data is insufficient to draw firm conclusions.
entries.context: This is among the first PRISMA-guided systematic reviews focused exclusively on topical glutathione in clinical dermatology, synthesizing the sparse human trial evidence base for a widely marketed ingredient.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human clinical trial evidence on topical glutathione

entries.title: Glutathione as a skin-lightening agent and in melasma: a systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: Glutathione Systematically Reviewed as Skin-Lightening Agent in Melasma
entries.summary: This systematic review evaluated published evidence on glutathione's efficacy and safety as a skin-lightening agent and melasma treatment across topical, oral, and IV formulations. Oral glutathione (250–500 mg/day) significantly reduced melanin index versus placebo; topical 0.5% outperformed lower concentrations; IV glutathione was contraindicated.
keyFindings: Five RCTs confirmed oral glutathione at 250–500 mg/day significantly reduced melanin index versus placebo.
Topical glutathione 0.5% was significantly more effective than 0.1% concentration and placebo.
Combination topical 2% plus oral glutathione was superior to either monotherapy alone.
IV glutathione showed no significant efficacy (37.5% vs. 18.7%, p=0.054) and is contraindicated due to side effects.
entries.limitations: Nearly equal numbers of included studies carried low and high risk of bias, limiting the strength of overall conclusions.
entries.context: This is the most comprehensive systematic review synthesizing RCT and clinical evidence on glutathione across all delivery routes for skin lightening and melasma, providing the clearest evidence-based guidance available for clinicians and researchers.
entries.landmarkReason: Definitive systematic review synthesizing human RCT evidence across all glutathione formulations

entries.title: Effect of long-term oral glutathione supplementation on gut microbiome of type 2 diabetic individuals.
entries.displayTitle: Oral Glutathione Supplementation Favorably Shifts Gut Microbiome in Type 2 Diabetes
entries.summary: This RCT examined 6 months of daily 500 mg oral glutathione supplementation in Indian type 2 diabetic individuals, analyzing gut microbiome via 16S rRNA sequencing. GSH supplementation reduced pathogenic bacteria and enriched beneficial genera while stabilizing HbA1c.
keyFindings: Phylum Proteobacteria abundance significantly decreased (P < 0.05) after 6 months of GSH supplementation.
Beneficial genera Megasphaera, Bacteroides, and Megamonas were significantly enriched (P < 0.05).
Pathogenic Escherichia/Shigella was significantly depleted (P < 0.05) following supplementation.
GSH supplementation also stabilized reduced HbA1c levels alongside antidiabetic treatment.
entries.limitations: Study conducted exclusively in Indian diabetic individuals, limiting generalizability; microbiome changes may not translate across ethnicities or dietary contexts.
entries.context: One of the few RCTs directly examining how oral glutathione supplementation affects the gut microbiome in a diabetic population, linking GSH's antioxidant role to meaningful microbial shifts relevant to metabolic disease management.
entries.landmarkReason: Randomized controlled trial directly testing the peptide

entries.title: Hexarelin alleviates apoptosis on ischemic acute kidney injury via MDM2/p53 pathway.
entries.displayTitle: Hexarelin Reduces Apoptosis in Ischemic Acute Kidney Injury via MDM2/p53
entries.studyType: Animal model — rat, with in vitro mechanistic study
entries.summary: This study investigated Hexarelin's protective effects in rat ischemia/reperfusion-induced acute kidney injury and HK-2 cell hypoxia/reoxygenation models. Hexarelin pretreatment improved renal function, reduced tubular necrosis, and inhibited apoptosis through MDM2/p53 pathway modulation.
keyFindings: Hexarelin pretreatment (100 µg/kg/day for 7 days) reduced serum creatinine and tubular necrosis in I/R-induced AKI rats.
Hexarelin downregulated pro-apoptotic Caspase-3, Bax, and Bad while upregulating anti-apoptotic Bcl-2.
Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinity between Hexarelin and MDM2, implicating the MDM2/p53 pathway.
In vitro H/R-induced HK-2 cell apoptosis was also significantly reduced by Hexarelin treatment.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to preclinical rat and cell-line models; no human pharmacokinetic or clinical validation data are provided.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to identify a molecular mechanism (MDM2/p53 pathway) by which Hexarelin protects against acute kidney injury, establishing a foundation for its potential therapeutic use in renal ischemia/reperfusion injury.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing primary pathway for Hexarelin's renal protective effect

entries.title: Hexarelin attenuates abdominal aortic aneurysm formation by inhibiting SMC phenotype switch and inflammasome activation.
entries.displayTitle: Hexarelin Reduces Aortic Aneurysm Formation in Mouse Model
entries.summary: This study tested hexarelin (200 µg/kg twice daily) in an elastase-induced mouse model of abdominal aortic aneurysm, finding it reduced aortic dilation, preserved elastin, and suppressed NLRP3 inflammasome and NF-κB inflammatory signaling.
keyFindings: Hexarelin decreased infrarenal aorta diameter in elastase-induced AAA mice.
Elastin degradation was improved and smooth muscle cell contractile phenotype was rescued with increased α-SMA.
MMP2 expression decreased in hexarelin-treated animals.
NLRP3 inflammasome activation, IL-18 production, and NF-κB signaling were suppressed by hexarelin.
entries.limitations: Mouse elastase-induced AAA model may not fully replicate human AAA pathophysiology; no human or larger-animal validation provided.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to examine hexarelin's role in AAA, extending its known cardiovascular protective effects to a new vascular pathology and identifying SMC phenotype switching and NF-κB/NLRP3 pathways as key mechanisms.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing hexarelin's role in AAA via SMC phenotype and inflammasome pathways

entries.title: Ghrelin receptor agonist hexarelin attenuates antinociceptive tolerance to morphine in rats.
entries.displayTitle: Hexarelin Reduces Morphine Tolerance and Enhances Analgesia in Rats
entries.journal: Canadian journal of physiology and pharmacology
entries.summary: This rat study evaluated hexarelin (a GHS-R agonist) combined with morphine on antinociception and tolerance using tail-flick and hot-plate tests. Co-administration of hexarelin with morphine enhanced analgesic effect and attenuated the development of morphine tolerance.
keyFindings: Hexarelin combined with morphine significantly attenuated analgesic tolerance compared to morphine alone.
Co-administration of hexarelin and morphine increased overall antinociceptive effect.
GHS-R antagonist [d-Lys3]-GHRP-6 showed no significant effect on morphine tolerance.
104 male Wistar rats used; tolerance induced via 3-day cumulative morphine dosing regimen.
entries.limitations: Rat model only; no human data, and long-term effects or mechanistic pathways underlying tolerance attenuation were not fully characterized.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to directly examine the role of ghrelin receptor agonism via hexarelin on morphine tolerance, providing mechanistic insight into GHS-R involvement in opioid antinociception pathways.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study — first direct evidence of hexarelin modulating morphine tolerance

entries.title: IGF-1 LR3 does not promote growth in late-gestation growth-restricted fetal sheep.
entries.displayTitle: IGF-1 LR3 Fails to Improve Growth in Growth-Restricted Fetal Sheep
entries.journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
entries.summary: A 1-week intravenous infusion of IGF-1 LR3 in growth-restricted fetal sheep (n=7 per group) did not improve fetal body weight, insulin, glucose, or glucose-stimulated insulin secretion compared to vehicle, though it did reduce circulating amino acids.
keyFindings: Fetal body weight was not significantly different between IGF-1 LR3 and vehicle groups after 1 week.
Insulin concentrations and glucose-stimulated insulin secretion were not attenuated by IGF-1 LR3 in FGR fetuses.
Circulating amino acids, including branched-chain amino acids, decreased significantly in the LR3 group (p=0.0232) but not vehicle.
Results contrast with prior normal fetal sheep data where IGF-1 LR3 increased body weight but suppressed insulin.
entries.limitations: Small group size (n=7 per group) and sheep fetal model limit direct translation to human fetal growth restriction.
entries.context: This is among the few controlled studies directly testing IGF-1 LR3 infusion in a placental insufficiency model, providing critical negative evidence that limits enthusiasm for IGF-1 LR3 as a standalone growth-promoting therapy in FGR.
entries.landmarkReason: Controlled mechanistic study in established FGR animal model; directly tests IGF-1 LR3 therapeutic mechanism

entries.title: Reduced glucose-stimulated insulin secretion following a 1-wk IGF-1 infusion in late gestation fetal sheep is due to an intrinsic islet defect.
entries.displayTitle: IGF-1 LR3 Infusion Impairs Fetal Islet Insulin Secretion in Sheep
entries.journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
entries.summary: Late-gestation fetal sheep received 1-week IGF-1 LR3 infusions; glucose-stimulated insulin secretion was reduced both in vivo and in isolated islets, indicating an intrinsic pancreatic beta-cell defect.
keyFindings: IGF-1 LR3 fetuses had significantly lower plasma insulin (p=0.0135) and glucose (p=0.0012) vs. controls.
In vivo glucose-stimulated insulin secretion was reduced in IGF-1 LR3 fetuses (p=0.0453).
Impaired insulin secretion persisted in isolated islets ex vivo (p=0.0447), confirming intrinsic defect.
No differences in total pancreatic insulin content were observed between groups.
entries.limitations: Small group sizes (n=8–9) and a sheep fetal model limit direct translation to human neonatal or adult physiology.
entries.context: This study provides mechanistic insight into how elevated IGF-1 — as seen in diabetic pregnancies — suppresses fetal beta-cell function, with implications for neonatal metabolic programming and long-term diabetes risk.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing IGF-1 LR3 effect on fetal beta-cell insulin secretion

entries.title: IGF-1 infusion to fetal sheep increases organ growth but not by stimulating nutrient transfer to the fetus.
entries.displayTitle: IGF-1 LR3 Infusion Increases Fetal Organ Growth Without Boosting Nutrient Transfer
entries.journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
entries.summary: Late-gestation fetal sheep received one week of intravenous LR3 IGF-1 or saline. Despite lower umbilical amino acid uptake and fetal plasma amino acid concentrations, LR3 IGF-1 increased heart, adrenal, and spleen weights and stimulated skeletal muscle myoblast proliferation.
keyFindings: Heart, adrenal gland, and spleen weights were significantly higher in LR3 IGF-1-treated fetuses (p < 0.05).
Umbilical amino acid uptake rates and fetal arterial amino acid concentrations were lower in LR3 IGF-1 group.
Uterine and umbilical blood flow and glucose, lactate, and oxygen uptake rates were unchanged.
Skeletal muscle myoblast proliferation was significantly higher in LR3 IGF-1-treated fetuses (p < 0.05).
entries.limitations: Small group sizes (n = 8 per group) and ovine fetal model limit direct translation to human IGF-1 biology and clinical dosing.
entries.context: This is one of the few controlled in vivo studies directly infusing LR3 IGF-1 into catheterized fetal sheep to dissect IGF-1's role in fetal organ growth versus placental nutrient transport, providing mechanistic clarity relevant to IGF-1 LR3 dosing and growth research.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study

entries.title: Attenuation of Visceral and Somatic Nociception by Ghrelin Mimetics.
entries.displayTitle: Ipamorelin Reduces Visceral and Somatic Pain in Rats
entries.summary: This rat study investigated whether ghrelin receptor agonists ipamorelin (peripherally restricted) and HM01 (centrally active) could attenuate non-inflammatory visceral hypersensitivity and somatic allodynia. Both mimetics significantly reduced colonic hypersensitivity and mechanical allodynia, effects blocked by a ghrelin receptor antagonist.
keyFindings: Ipamorelin significantly attenuated colonic hypersensitivity measured by visceromotor response to colorectal distension (0–60 mmHg).
Both ghrelin mimetics reduced somatic mechanical allodynia quantified by von Frey filament paw withdrawal counts.
Anti-nociceptive effects were fully reversed by ghrelin receptor antagonist H0900, confirming receptor-mediated mechanism.
Peripheral ghrelin receptor activation alone was sufficient to attenuate both visceral and somatic pain.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to a rat model; translation to human pain conditions and clinical dosing has not been established.
entries.context: This study is among the first to demonstrate that a peripherally restricted ghrelin mimetic (ipamorelin) can attenuate non-inflammatory visceral and somatic pain, establishing a mechanistic basis for ipamorelin's potential analgesic applications.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing ipamorelin's anti-nociceptive mechanism via peripheral ghrelin receptor activation

entries.title: Kisspeptin-10 binding to Gpr54 in osteoclasts prevents bone loss by activating Dusp18-mediated dephosphorylation of Src.
entries.displayTitle: Kisspeptin-10 Activates Dusp18 to Suppress Osteoclasts and Prevent Bone Loss
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse (mechanistic + in vivo intervention)
entries.summary: This study used mouse knockout models and in vivo administration to show that Kisspeptin-10 (Kp-10) binding to GPR54 upregulates the phosphatase Dusp18, which dephosphorylates Src at Tyr416 in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption and preventing age-related bone loss.
keyFindings: Kp-10 binding to GPR54 recruits Dusp18 to dephosphorylate Src at Tyr416, inactivating osteoclasts.
Kiss1, Gpr54, and Dusp18 knockout mice all displayed osteoclast hyperactivation and significant bone loss.
In vivo Kp-10 administration abrogated bone loss by suppressing osteoclast activity.
GPR54's C-terminal proline/arginine-rich motif is essential for co-recruiting active Src and Dusp18.
entries.limitations: Findings are based on mouse knockout and in vivo models; no human clinical data are presented to confirm translational relevance.
entries.context: This is the first study to establish a direct mechanistic link between Kisspeptin-10/GPR54 signaling and osteoclast regulation via Src dephosphorylation, identifying a novel therapeutic target for osteoporosis.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing primary therapeutic mechanism

entries.title: The roles of kisspeptin and neurokinin B in GnRH pulse generation in humans, and their potential clinical application.
entries.displayTitle: Continuous Kisspeptin-10 Infusion Drives LH Pulses Without Desensitization
entries.summary: This review synthesizes clinical studies showing that continuous receptor-saturating infusion of kisspeptin-10 increases LH pulse frequency in healthy men and women, hypogonadal patients, and those with NKB pathway mutations — without desensitization over 22 hours.
keyFindings: Continuous kisspeptin-10 infusion raised LH pulse frequency across multiple human populations including healthy and hypogonadal subjects.
No receptor desensitization was observed after 22 hours of continuous receptor-saturating kisspeptin-10 infusion in humans.
Pulsatile kisspeptin secretion is not required for GnRH pulse generation in humans, contrasting rodent data.
Effects were demonstrated in women with PCOS and in subjects with NKB/NK3R gene mutations.
entries.limitations: Synthesizes authors' own clinical data rather than a systematic meta-analysis; independent replication across other centers is limited.
entries.context: This paper challenges the prevailing rodent-based model of kisspeptin pulse generation and consolidates human clinical evidence redefining how kisspeptin-10 drives reproductive hormone pulses — foundational for therapeutic development.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic synthesis of human clinical trials establishing mechanism and therapeutic relevance

entries.title: Kisspeptin and neurokinin B interactions in modulating gonadotropin secretion in women with polycystic ovary syndrome.
entries.displayTitle: NKB Antagonism Reduces LH Pulses but Preserves Kisspeptin Response in PCOS
entries.summary: This randomized controlled trial tested a neurokinin 3 receptor antagonist (MLE4901) with or without kisspeptin-10 infusion in women with PCOS. NK3R antagonism reduced LH pulse frequency and FSH, while the stimulatory LH response to kisspeptin-10 was preserved, revealing interactive NKB–kisspeptin regulation of GnRH secretion.
keyFindings: NK3Ra reduced mean LH from 6.5 to 4.0 IU/l and LH pulse frequency from 0.8 to 0.5 pulses/h (P<0.05).
FSH secretion was also reduced by NK3Ra (2.5 to 2.0 IU/l, P<0.05).
Kisspeptin-10 infusion increased LH secretion both with and without NK3Ra pre-treatment.
After NK3Ra, the positive correlation between kisspeptin-10 LH response and oestradiol was abolished.
entries.limitations: Very small sample size (n=15 total) limits generalizability; dose-response of NK3R antagonism and impact of obesity/PCOS phenotype variability were not evaluated.
entries.context: This is one of the first RCTs to dissect the NKB–kisspeptin interaction specifically in PCOS, directly informing therapeutic targeting of the KNDy neuroendocrine axis for reproductive dysfunction.
entries.landmarkReason: Randomized controlled trial directly testing kisspeptin-10 in human PCOS

entries.title: Lysine-Proline-Valine peptide mitigates fine dust-induced keratinocyte apoptosis and inflammation by regulating oxidative stress and modulating the MAPK/NF-κB pathway.
entries.displayTitle: KPV Peptide Reduces Fine Dust-Induced Skin Cell Inflammation and Death
entries.summary: This in vitro study examined KPV's protective effects against fine particulate matter (PM2.5) in human HaCaT keratinocytes and a 3D skin model. KPV at 50 µg/mL restored cell viability, reduced IL-1β secretion, and suppressed ROS-driven MAPK/NF-κB signaling and caspase-1-mediated pyroptosis.
keyFindings: KPV at 50 µg/mL restored HaCaT cell viability suppressed by PM2.5 cytotoxicity.
KPV reduced IL-1β secretion by blocking ROS-mediated caspase-1 activation.
KPV suppressed apoptosis markers Bax, Bcl-2, and cleaved caspase-3 via NF-κB inhibition.
KPV attenuated PM-induced inflammatory cell death in a 3D human skin model.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to cell culture and a 3D skin model; no in vivo animal or human data were generated to confirm efficacy or relevant dosing.
entries.context: This study maps KPV's anti-inflammatory mechanism in environmentally stressed keratinocytes, linking its known NF-κB suppression to pyroptosis inhibition — extending understanding of KPV's skin-protective pathway relevant to pollution-related dermatology.
entries.landmarkReason: Mechanistic study establishing KPV's MAPK/NF-κB/pyroptosis pathway in skin inflammation

entries.title: Mitochondrial-Encoded Peptide MOTS-c, Diabetes, and Aging-Related Diseases.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c Mitochondrial Peptide Regulates Diabetes and Aging Disease
entries.summary: This review synthesizes published evidence on MOTS-c, a mitochondrial-encoded peptide, and its role in regulating cellular metabolism, insulin action, and age-related diseases including type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
keyFindings: MOTS-c is encoded in the mitochondrial 12S rRNA open reading frame and regulates cellular metabolism.
MOTS-c influences insulin sensitivity and is implicated in type 1 and type 2 diabetes pathophysiology.
Related mitochondrial peptides (humanin, SHLPs, SHMOOSE) share metabolic regulatory roles in aging diseases.
MOTS-c is presented as a potential therapeutic target for diabetes and senescence-related conditions.
entries.limitations: Narrative reviews lack systematic search methodology and are subject to selection bias in the studies chosen for inclusion.
entries.context: As a focused review of MOTS-c's role in diabetes from a leading metabolism journal with NIH support, this article consolidates the mechanistic and translational evidence base guiding MOTS-c therapeutic research.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic synthesis of human and animal evidence on MOTS-c mechanism and diabetes relevance

entries.title: Mitochondria-derived peptide MOTS-c: effects and mechanisms related to stress, metabolism and aging.
entries.displayTitle: MOTS-c Regulates Metabolism, Stress Response, and Aging Mechanisms
entries.summary: This review synthesizes published evidence on MOTS-c, a mitochondria-derived peptide encoded by 12S rRNA, covering its nuclear translocation, AMPK pathway activation, and roles in energy metabolism, insulin resistance, inflammation, and aging-related pathology.
keyFindings: MOTS-c translocates to the nucleus under stress or exercise, regulating ARE-driven stress adaptation genes.
Primary mechanism operates via the Folate-AICAR-AMPK pathway, influencing systemic energy homeostasis.
MOTS-c modulates insulin resistance, inflammatory response, and aging-related pathological processes.
Circulating MOTS-c levels respond dynamically to exercise and metabolic stress states.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review rather than systematic review or meta-analysis, it is subject to selection bias and does not quantitatively pool effect sizes across studies.
entries.context: This is one of the most comprehensive reviews synthesizing the mechanistic and translational literature on MOTS-c, consolidating nuclear retrograde signaling, metabolic regulation, and aging biology into a single reference framework for researchers.
entries.landmarkReason: Comprehensive mechanistic review synthesizing primary evidence on MOTS-c biology

entries.title: Mitochondrial-derived peptides in energy metabolism.
entries.displayTitle: Mitochondrial-Derived Peptides Regulate Energy Metabolism and Insulin Sensitivity
entries.journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
entries.summary: This review synthesizes evidence on mitochondrial-derived peptides (MDPs), including MOTS-c, as metabolic signal transducers. It examines how metabolic conditions alter circulating MDP levels and how exogenous MDP treatment improves insulin sensitivity and protects against age-related metabolic disorders in rodents.
keyFindings: Obesity, diabetes, and aging are associated with lower circulating MDP levels in humans.
Muscle MDP expression is upregulated by exercise and mitochondrial stress in humans.
MOTS-c, humanin, and SHLP2 treatment enhances insulin sensitivity in rodent models.
mtDNA variants alter MDP function, supporting MDPs as metabolic signal transducers in humans.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review without meta-analytic methods, it is subject to selection bias and does not quantitatively pool effect sizes.
entries.context: This is a comprehensive synthesis of the mechanistic and translational evidence for MOTS-c and related MDPs in metabolic regulation, representing the primary scholarly framework researchers reference when studying MOTS-c's therapeutic potential.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic synthesis of human and animal evidence on MOTS-c mechanism and metabolic role

entries.title: The Safety and Antiaging Effects of Nicotinamide Mononucleotide in Human Clinical Trials: an Update.
entries.displayTitle: NMN Supplementation Safety and Antiaging Effects: Human Trials Review
entries.summary: This 2023 review synthesizes current human clinical trial evidence on NMN supplementation, summarizing its safety profile and potential antiaging effects, while contextualizing findings within NAD+ biology from cell and animal models.
keyFindings: NAD+ levels decline with age in skin, blood, liver, muscle, and brain.
NMN supplementation increases NAD+ concentrations in human clinical trials.
Preclinical studies show NMN mitigates oxidative stress, DNA damage, neurodegeneration, and inflammation.
A dozen human clinical trials with NMN were underway as of 2023.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review rather than systematic meta-analysis, it lacks pooled quantitative synthesis and may be subject to selection bias in study inclusion.
entries.context: This review provides one of the most comprehensive summaries of human NMN clinical trial progress available, making it a key reference for understanding where human evidence stands on NAD+ precursor supplementation.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human clinical trial evidence

entries.title: The efficacy and safety of β-nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) supplementation in healthy middle-aged adults: a randomized, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, dose-dependent clinical trial.
entries.displayTitle: NMN Raises NAD Levels and Improves Physical Performance in Humans
entries.studyType: Randomized controlled trial — multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled
entries.summary: This 60-day randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 80 healthy middle-aged adults tested 300, 600, and 900 mg daily oral NMN. All doses significantly increased blood NAD concentrations and improved six-minute walk distance versus placebo, with 600 mg appearing optimal.
keyFindings: All NMN doses significantly increased blood NAD vs. placebo and baseline at days 30 and 60 (all p ≤ 0.001).
Six-minute walking distance increased significantly in all NMN groups vs. placebo at days 30 and 60 (all p < 0.01).
Biological age rose significantly in placebo group but remained stable in all NMN-treated groups (all p < 0.05).
NMN was well tolerated up to 900 mg/day with no safety signals across laboratory or clinical measures.
entries.limitations: Small sample size (n=80 across four arms) and short 60-day duration limit conclusions about long-term efficacy and safety.
entries.context: One of the first multicenter RCTs to demonstrate dose-dependent NAD elevation and functional performance benefits of oral NMN in humans, directly informing dosing strategies used in practice.
entries.landmarkReason: First multicenter dose-finding RCT of NMN in humans with functional outcomes

entries.title: Nicotinamide mononucleotide increases muscle insulin sensitivity in prediabetic women.
entries.displayTitle: NMN Supplementation Improves Muscle Insulin Sensitivity in Prediabetic Women
entries.summary: A 10-week randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind trial in postmenopausal women with prediabetes found that oral NMN supplementation significantly increased skeletal muscle insulin sensitivity and insulin signaling compared to placebo.
keyFindings: NMN supplementation increased insulin-stimulated glucose disposal measured by hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp.
NMN enhanced skeletal muscle insulin signaling via AKT and mTOR phosphorylation; placebo showed no change.
NMN up-regulated platelet-derived growth factor receptor β and genes related to muscle remodeling.
Trial duration was 10 weeks in overweight/obese postmenopausal women with prediabetes (NCT03151239).
entries.limitations: Single-sex, small sample of postmenopausal overweight women limits generalizability to broader populations.
entries.context: This is one of the first rigorous human RCTs demonstrating that boosting NAD+ biosynthesis via NMN has meaningful metabolic effects in people, translating decades of rodent data into clinical evidence.
entries.landmarkReason: First human RCT

entries.title: Small Effects, Questionable Outcomes: Bremelanotide for Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder.
entries.displayTitle: Bremelanotide Phase III Benefits Modest With Questionable Outcome Validity
entries.studyType: Systematic review / reanalysis of Phase 3 RCT data
entries.summary: This review reanalyzed the RECONNECT phase III trials of bremelanotide (PT-141) for HSDD in women, examining measurement validity of efficacy outcomes. Effect sizes ranged from nil to small, and most apparently positive outcomes were derived post-hoc using inadequately validated instruments.
keyFindings: 8 of 11 pre-specified efficacy outcomes from RECONNECT trials were previously unpublished.
Effect sizes across unpublished outcomes ranged from nil to small.
Primary efficacy measures (FSFI, FSDS-DAO) have questionable validity for women with HSDD.
Nearly all outcomes showing modest benefit were likely derived post-hoc.
entries.limitations: Study is a secondary reanalysis and critique rather than an independent trial, limiting ability to draw conclusions about true efficacy.
entries.context: The RECONNECT trials formed the pivotal evidence base for FDA approval of bremelanotide; this reanalysis critically challenges the validity of the outcome measures and completeness of reporting, directly informing how the trial evidence should be interpreted.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review reanalyzing Phase 3 trial data — primary regulatory evidence

entries.title: Safety Profile of Bremelanotide Across the Clinical Development Program.
entries.displayTitle: Bremelanotide Phase 3 Safety Profile Across 3,500 Subjects
entries.summary: This review analyzed the full clinical development program (phases 1–3) of bremelanotide (PT-141) across 43 studies and 3,500 subjects, characterizing its safety profile through 18 months of use in premenopausal women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
keyFindings: Nausea occurred in 40.0% of bremelanotide vs. 1.3% of placebo subjects — the most common adverse event.
Flushing (20.3% vs. 1.3%) and headache (11.3% vs. 1.9%) were the next most frequent adverse events.
Focal hyperpigmentation occurred in >one-third of subjects with up to 16 consecutive daily dosings.
Small but statistically significant transient blood pressure increases observed; no deaths reported.
entries.limitations: Higher discontinuation rate in bremelanotide group (30% vs. 13% placebo) limits long-term tolerability conclusions, and the all-female premenopausal population restricts generalizability.
entries.context: As the comprehensive safety review spanning the entire FDA approval program for bremelanotide, this is the definitive reference for clinicians and researchers assessing the risk profile of PT-141 in its approved indication.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 trial — primary safety evidence across full clinical development program

entries.title: Re-Analyzing Phase III Bremelanotide Trials for "Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder" in Women.
entries.displayTitle: Meta-Analysis Finds Bremelanotide Benefits Modest, Reporting Incomplete
entries.summary: This meta-analysis re-analyzed FDA New Drug Application data from two Phase III trials of bremelanotide (PT-141) for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in women, finding modest efficacy alongside high adverse-event discontinuation rates and participant preference for placebo.
keyFindings: 72.72% of protocol-listed outcomes were not reported in the original Kingsberg et al. publication.
Adverse event-induced discontinuation was substantially higher on bremelanotide: OR = 11.98, NNH = 6.
Participants significantly preferred placebo over bremelanotide: OR = 0.30, NNH = 4.
No secondary efficacy outcome had a stated rationale or cited evidence of validity.
entries.limitations: Analysis relies on FDA NDA data rather than individual patient data, and cannot fully adjudicate outcome selection bias in the original trials.
entries.context: This is the primary independent meta-analysis of the Phase III trial data underlying bremelanotide's FDA approval, revealing critical reporting gaps and safety signals not disclosed in the original publication.
entries.landmarkReason: Definitive meta-analysis of Phase III trial data

entries.title: Efficacy and Safety of Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists for Weight Loss Among Adults Without Diabetes : A Systematic Review of Randomized Controlled Trials.
entries.displayTitle: GLP-1 Agonists Achieve Up to 22% Weight Loss in Non-Diabetic Adults
entries.summary: This systematic review of 26 placebo-controlled RCTs (15,491 participants) evaluated GLP-1 receptor agonists and co-agonists for obesity in adults without diabetes, finding retatrutide produced the greatest weight loss at 22.1% over 48 weeks, with predominantly gastrointestinal adverse events.
keyFindings: Retatrutide 12 mg weekly achieved 22.1% weight loss (95% CI, 19.3%–24.9%) at 48 weeks — highest among all agents.
Tirzepatide 15 mg weekly produced 17.8% weight loss; semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly produced 13.9% weight loss.
Gastrointestinal adverse events occurred in 47%–84% of GLP-1 RA recipients vs. 13%–63% on placebo.
Serious adverse events were rare (0%–10% active vs. 0%–12% placebo); most AEs were non-life-threatening.
entries.limitations: No head-to-head RCTs were available, and heterogeneity across trials prevented formal meta-analysis, limiting direct comparative conclusions.
entries.context: This is the most comprehensive systematic review to date comparing approved and investigational GLP-1-based agents for obesity in non-diabetic adults, directly contextualizing retatrutide's efficacy against established competitors.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human RCT evidence including retatrutide

entries.title: Emerging pharmacotherapies for obesity: A systematic review.
entries.displayTitle: Systematic Review: Retatrutide Among Most Promising Emerging Obesity Therapies
entries.summary: This systematic review of 53 phase 2 and phase 3 trials (2012–2024) evaluated novel antiobesity pharmacotherapies in adults with overweight/obesity. Incretin-based therapies, including the triple agonist retatrutide, showed the most promising weight loss results, with phase 2 mean weight reductions of 7.4%–24.2%.
keyFindings: 53 phase 2 and phase 3 trials identified across 36 emerging antiobesity drugs or combinations.
Phase 2 incretin-based therapies achieved mean weight loss of 7.4%–24.2%.
Retatrutide (GLP-1/GIP/glucagon triple agonist) is in ongoing phase 3 trials.
Nearly half of drugs in phase 2 trials are incretin analogs, representing the most active pipeline segment.
entries.limitations: Retatrutide phase 3 data are not yet available; findings reflect pipeline status and phase 2 results rather than definitive efficacy or safety conclusions.
entries.context: This is the most comprehensive recent synthesis of the obesity drug pipeline, contextualizing retatrutide's phase 3 status and triple-agonist mechanism within the broader landscape of emerging therapies — essential reading for researchers tracking retatrutide's clinical development.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing phase 2 and phase 3 human trial evidence

entries.title: Triple-Hormone-Receptor Agonist Retatrutide for Obesity - A Phase 2 Trial.
entries.displayTitle: Retatrutide Cuts Body Weight Up to 24% in Phase 2 RCT
entries.journal: The New England journal of medicine
entries.summary: This phase 2 double-blind RCT tested six dose regimens of retatrutide versus placebo in 338 adults with obesity over 48 weeks. The highest doses (8 mg and 12 mg) produced mean body weight reductions of ~23–24%, with 100% of participants achieving ≥5% weight loss.
keyFindings: At 48 weeks, 12 mg retatrutide produced a mean body weight reduction of 24.2% vs. 2.1% for placebo.
100% of 8 mg and 12 mg participants achieved ≥5% weight loss; 83% on 12 mg lost ≥15%.
Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most common, dose-related, and mostly mild-to-moderate.
Dose-dependent heart rate increases peaked at 24 weeks and declined thereafter.
entries.limitations: 48-week duration and moderate sample size (n=338) limit conclusions about long-term safety, cardiovascular outcomes, and weight maintenance after discontinuation.
entries.context: This is the pivotal phase 2 trial establishing retatrutide's dose-response profile for obesity, demonstrating weight loss magnitudes exceeding most existing approved therapies and informing dose selection for phase 3 development.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 2 RCT — primary efficacy and safety evidence

entries.title: Weight regain and cardiometabolic effects after withdrawal of semaglutide: The STEP 1 trial extension.
entries.displayTitle: Two-Thirds of Semaglutide Weight Loss Regained After Withdrawal
entries.summary: The STEP 1 extension followed 327 adults one year after stopping semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly. Participants regained approximately two-thirds of lost weight, and cardiometabolic improvements largely reverted toward baseline.
keyFindings: Mean weight loss of 17.3% with semaglutide at week 68 fell to net 5.6% by week 120.
Semaglutide participants regained 11.6 percentage points of lost weight within one year of stopping.
Placebo group regained only 1.9 percentage points after lifestyle intervention withdrawal.
Cardiometabolic improvements (blood pressure, lipids, glycemic markers) largely reversed after cessation.
entries.limitations: Extension subset (n=327) was not fully representative of the parent trial, and lifestyle support was also withdrawn simultaneously, complicating isolation of drug effects.
entries.context: This extension of the pivotal STEP 1 trial (n=1961) provides the primary controlled evidence on what happens when semaglutide is discontinued, directly shaping clinical guidance on chronic obesity treatment.
entries.landmarkReason: RCT extension — primary evidence for post-withdrawal weight regain with semaglutide

entries.title: Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide Beats Semaglutide on HbA1c and Weight Loss
entries.journal: The New England journal of medicine
entries.summary: This 40-week phase 3 RCT (n=1,879) compared tirzepatide (5, 10, 15 mg) to semaglutide 1 mg weekly in type 2 diabetes. All tirzepatide doses were non-inferior and superior to semaglutide for HbA1c reduction, with greater weight loss across all doses.
keyFindings: HbA1c reductions: −2.01%, −2.24%, −2.30% (tirzepatide) vs. −1.86% (semaglutide).
Tirzepatide superior to semaglutide at all doses (p≤0.02) for glycemic control.
Body weight reductions greater with tirzepatide: −1.9 kg, −3.6 kg, −5.5 kg vs. semaglutide.
Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most common; hypoglycemia rates were low in both groups.
entries.limitations: Open-label design may introduce performance bias; semaglutide dose (1 mg) did not include the 2 mg option available in some markets.
entries.context: SURPASS-2 is the pivotal head-to-head trial establishing tirzepatide's superiority over the leading GLP-1 agonist semaglutide, directly informing clinical positioning of both agents in type 2 diabetes management.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 RCT — primary comparative evidence for semaglutide vs. dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist

entries.title: Once-Weekly Semaglutide in Adults with Overweight or Obesity.
entries.displayTitle: Weekly Semaglutide 2.4 mg Cuts Body Weight 15% in Obesity
entries.journal: The New England journal of medicine
entries.summary: This 68-week double-blind RCT enrolled 1,961 non-diabetic adults with obesity, randomizing them 2:1 to once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg or placebo plus lifestyle intervention. Semaglutide produced a mean 14.9% body weight reduction versus 2.4% with placebo.
keyFindings: Mean body weight reduction: -14.9% semaglutide vs. -2.4% placebo (treatment difference -12.4 percentage points, P<0.001).
86.4% of semaglutide participants lost ≥5% body weight vs. 31.5% on placebo.
50.5% of semaglutide participants achieved ≥15% weight loss vs. only 4.9% on placebo.
Nausea and diarrhea were most common adverse events; 4.5% discontinued semaglutide due to GI events.
entries.limitations: Trial duration of 68 weeks limits conclusions about long-term weight maintenance and safety beyond the study period.
entries.context: The STEP 1 trial is the pivotal Phase 3 RCT that established semaglutide 2.4 mg as a breakthrough pharmacologic option for obesity, directly supporting its FDA approval for chronic weight management.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 RCT — primary evidence for semaglutide obesity indication

entries.title: Antidepressant-like and antistress effects of the ACTH(4-10) synthetic analogs Semax and Melanotan II on male rats in a model of chronic unpredictable stress.
entries.displayTitle: Semax Reverses Anhedonia and Stress Markers in Rat Depression Model
entries.summary: Male rats subjected to chronic unpredictable stress received daily intraperitoneal Semax (60 nmol/kg) or Melanotan II. Both peptides reversed CUS-induced anhedonia, body weight suppression, adrenal hypertrophy, and reduced hippocampal BDNF levels.
keyFindings: Semax reversed CUS-induced anhedonia as measured by sucrose preference test.
Semax attenuated adrenal hypertrophy, a marker of chronic HPA axis overactivation.
Hippocampal BDNF levels, reduced by CUS, were restored by Semax treatment.
No effects detected on forced swim test immobility for either peptide or CUS procedure.
entries.limitations: Study used only male rats at a single dose, limiting generalizability across sexes and dose ranges.
entries.context: This study provides mechanistic evidence linking Semax's antidepressant-like effects to HPA axis normalization and hippocampal BDNF restoration, supporting its therapeutic rationale for stress-related disorders.
entries.landmarkReason: Mechanistic animal study establishing antidepressant pathway for Semax via HPA axis and BDNF

entries.title: Novel Insights into the Protective Properties of ACTHPGP (Semax) Peptide at the Transcriptome Level Following Cerebral Ischaemia-Reperfusion in Rats.
entries.displayTitle: Semax Reverses Ischaemia-Reperfusion Gene Expression in Rat Brain
entries.summary: Using RNA-Seq in a rat transient middle cerebral artery occlusion (tMCAO) model, researchers identified 394 differentially expressed genes in Semax-treated versus saline-treated animals 24 hours post-ischaemia, revealing that Semax suppresses inflammation-related genes and restores neurotransmission-related gene expression.
keyFindings: 394 differentially expressed genes (>1.5-fold change) identified in Semax-treated vs. saline-treated tMCAO rats.
Semax suppressed expression of inflammation-related genes upregulated by ischaemia-reperfusion.
Semax activated neurotransmission-related genes suppressed during ischaemia-reperfusion.
Neuroprotection may involve compensating mRNA expression patterns disrupted by ischaemia.
entries.limitations: Animal model findings may not directly translate to human ischaemic stroke; sample size and single time-point limit mechanistic conclusions.
entries.context: This is one of the first genome-wide transcriptomic analyses of Semax's neuroprotective mechanism, providing molecular-level insight into how a clinically used peptide counteracts ischaemic brain injury.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing transcriptomic basis of Semax neuroprotection

entries.title: GHRH agonist MR-409 protects β-cells from streptozotocin-induced diabetes.
entries.displayTitle: GHRH Agonist MR-409 Protects Beta-Cells in Type 1 Diabetes Models
entries.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse + in vitro mechanistic study
entries.summary: This study examined the GHRH receptor agonist MR-409 in streptozotocin-induced diabetic mice and human islets. MR-409 activated the cAMP/PKA/CREB/IRS2 survival pathway, reduced cytokine-mediated beta-cell death, improved insulin secretion, and preserved beta-cell mass in vivo.
keyFindings: MR-409 induced IRS2 expression via cAMP/PKA/CREB axis in mouse and human islets.
Treatment reduced beta-cell death and improved insulin secretion under proinflammatory cytokine stress.
STZ-treated mice given MR-409 showed better glucose homeostasis and higher insulin levels.
Beta-cell mass was preserved in MR-409-treated mice compared to untreated STZ controls.
entries.limitations: MR-409 is a synthetic GHRH analog distinct from Sermorelin; no direct Sermorelin data or human clinical outcomes are reported.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to define the cytoprotective mechanism of GHRH receptor agonists on pancreatic beta-cells in a T1D model, establishing IRS2 signaling as a key pathway — directly relevant to Sermorelin's receptor class.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study for GHRH agonist class beta-cell protection

entries.title: Growth hormone-releasing hormone agonists ameliorate chronic kidney disease-induced heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.
entries.displayTitle: GHRH Agonist Improves Diastolic Function in CKD-Induced HFpEF
entries.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
entries.summary: In a porcine 5/6 nephrectomy model of CKD-induced HFpEF, subcutaneous GHRH agonist (MR-409) for 4–6 weeks normalized end-diastolic pressure, improved diastolic relaxation, and reduced pro-BNP compared to placebo.
keyFindings: GHRH-A normalized end-diastolic pressure vs. placebo (p = 0.03) in HFpEF pigs.
EDP/EDV ratio significantly reduced after treatment (p = 0.018).
Cardiomyocyte calcium transient amplitude increased with GHRH-A (p = 0.009).
Titin isoform ratio improved, indicating favorable myocardial structural remodeling (p = 0.0022).
entries.limitations: Study used MR-409, a synthetic GHRH analog, not sermorelin specifically; female pigs only, limiting generalizability across sexes and species.
entries.context: HFpEF lacks effective therapies; this large-animal randomized study provides mechanistic and hemodynamic evidence that GHRH agonists (related to sermorelin's class) meaningfully reverse CKD-driven diastolic dysfunction, supporting translation to human trials.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study in large-animal model establishing GHRH agonist class effect on HFpEF

entries.title: [Pharmacological Activation of ERRα/β/γ as an Exercise Mimetic: Potential Therapeutic Applications].
entries.displayTitle: Pan-ERR Agonists Like SLU-PP-332 Mimic Exercise Metabolically
entries.studyType: Systematic review — preclinical (animal and in vitro)
entries.summary: A systematic review of 2020–2024 preclinical literature evaluated pan-ERR agonists, particularly SLU-PP-332 and SLU-PP-915, as exercise mimetics. These compounds induced aerobic exercise-like gene expression, enhanced fatty acid oxidation, reduced adiposity, and improved glycemic control in animal and cell models.
keyFindings: Pan-ERR agonists activated an ERRα-dependent gene program resembling acute aerobic exercise, including Ddit4 and Slc25a25.
SLU-PP-332 increased type IIa oxidative-glycolytic muscle fibers and improved endurance in preclinical models.
Both compounds reduced adiposity, improved glycemic control, and raised basal energy expenditure in obesity models without evident toxicity.
ERR agonists restored mitochondrial function and reduced inflammation in aging kidney models.
entries.limitations: All included studies are preclinical; no human clinical trials have been conducted, leaving efficacy and safety in humans unconfirmed.
entries.context: This is the most comprehensive systematic synthesis to date of SLU-PP-332's mechanistic and physiological effects, consolidating preclinical evidence for its candidacy as a therapeutic exercise mimetic.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing primary preclinical evidence on SLU-PP-332 as exercise mimetic

entries.title: A Synthetic ERR Agonist Alleviates Metabolic Syndrome.
entries.displayTitle: SLU-PP-332 Reduces Fat Mass and Improves Insulin Sensitivity in Obese Mice
entries.journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics
entries.summary: SLU-PP-332, an ERR agonist with exercise-mimetic activity, was tested in diet-induced obese mouse models. Treatment increased energy expenditure and fatty acid oxidation while decreasing fat mass and improving insulin sensitivity.
keyFindings: SLU-PP-332 increased whole-body energy expenditure in diet-induced obese mice.
Treatment reduced fat mass accumulation in mouse models of obesity.
Improved insulin sensitivity observed in metabolic syndrome mouse models.
Pharmacological ERR activation mimicked aerobic exercise metabolic adaptations.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to mouse models; human pharmacokinetics, safety, and efficacy remain untested.
entries.context: This study is among the first to demonstrate that pharmacological ERR agonism via SLU-PP-332 can replicate exercise-induced metabolic benefits, establishing it as a candidate exercise mimetic for obesity and metabolic syndrome treatment.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing primary therapeutic mechanism in relevant disease models

entries.title: Synthetic ERRα/β/γ Agonist Induces an ERRα-Dependent Acute Aerobic Exercise Response and Enhances Exercise Capacity.
entries.displayTitle: SLU-PP-332 Acts as ERR Pan-Agonist, Boosting Exercise Endurance in Mice
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse; in vitro mechanistic study
entries.summary: This study identified SLU-PP-332 as a synthetic pan-agonist targeting all three ERR subtypes (α, β, γ) with highest potency for ERRα. In mouse models, it increased oxidative muscle fibers, enhanced exercise endurance, and induced an ERRα-dependent aerobic exercise gene program.
keyFindings: SLU-PP-332 is the first synthetic ERR pan-agonist with highest potency for ERRα.
Compound increased type IIa oxidative skeletal muscle fibers in mice.
SLU-PP-332 enhanced exercise endurance in mice via ERRα-specific activation.
Increased mitochondrial function and cellular respiration in skeletal muscle cell line.
entries.limitations: Findings are limited to mouse models and cell lines; no human pharmacokinetic or efficacy data are reported.
entries.context: This is the foundational study identifying SLU-PP-332 and establishing its mechanism as an exercise mimetic through ERRα activation — the primary basis for its investigation as a metabolic and performance-enhancing compound.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study — first identification of SLU-PP-332 and its primary exercise-mimetic mechanism

entries.title: Elamipretide: First Approval.
entries.displayTitle: Elamipretide Receives First FDA Approval for Barth Syndrome
entries.summary: This review summarizes the development milestones of elamipretide (SS-31), a mitochondrial cardiolipin binder, culminating in its 2025 FDA accelerated approval for improving muscle strength in Barth syndrome patients — the first disease-specific treatment for this ultra-rare condition.
keyFindings: FDA granted accelerated approval in September 2025 for Barth syndrome patients ≥30 kg.
Elamipretide is the first approved disease-specific treatment for Barth syndrome.
Phase III trials ongoing for dry age-related macular degeneration and mitochondrial myopathies.
Mechanism involves binding mitochondrial cardiolipin to stabilize mitochondrial function.
entries.limitations: As a review summarizing approval milestones, it does not present original clinical trial data or effect sizes directly.
entries.context: This is the landmark regulatory milestone for SS-31/elamipretide, marking the first FDA approval of a mitochondria-targeting peptide and establishing clinical proof-of-concept for cardiolipin-binding therapy in a rare genetic disease.
entries.landmarkReason: First regulatory approval — phase 3 development and accelerated FDA approval documented

entries.title: Elamipretide: A Review of Its Structure, Mechanism of Action, and Therapeutic Potential.
entries.displayTitle: Elamipretide (SS-31): Structure, Mechanism, and Clinical Trial Overview
entries.summary: This review synthesizes preclinical and clinical evidence on Elamipretide (SS-31), a mitochondria-targeting tetrapeptide that binds cardiolipin to stabilize cristae, reduce oxidative stress, and enhance ATP production, covering trials including PROGRESS-HF, TAZPOWER, MMPOWER-3, and ReCLAIM.
keyFindings: SS-31 selectively binds cardiolipin in the inner mitochondrial membrane, stabilizing cristae architecture.
Preclinical models show efficacy in heart failure, neurodegeneration, ischemia-reperfusion injury, and muscle atrophy.
Multiple named clinical trials (PROGRESS-HF, TAZPOWER, MMPOWER-3, ReCLAIM) have evaluated SS-31 in humans.
Mechanism centers on reducing oxidative stress and restoring mitochondrial bioenergetics across diverse disease models.
entries.limitations: As a narrative review rather than a systematic review or meta-analysis, it may be subject to selection bias in the studies discussed.
entries.context: This is a comprehensive synthesis of the mechanistic and clinical landscape for SS-31, serving as a key reference that consolidates foundational mechanism studies and multiple human clinical trials into a single authoritative overview.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human and animal evidence

entries.title: Long-term efficacy and safety of elamipretide in patients with Barth syndrome: 168-week open-label extension results of TAZPOWER.
entries.displayTitle: Elamipretide Improves Walking and Cardiac Function Over 168 Weeks in Barth Syndrome
entries.journal: Genetics in medicine : official journal of the American College of Medical Genetics
entries.summary: This 168-week open-label extension of the TAZPOWER RCT evaluated daily subcutaneous elamipretide (40 mg) in 10 Barth syndrome patients. Sustained improvements were seen in 6-minute walk distance, fatigue scores, cardiac volumes, and cardiolipin biomarkers.
keyFindings: Cumulative 96.1 m improvement in 6-minute walk test at week 168 (P = .003).
BTHS-SA Total Fatigue scores remained below baseline at all OLE time points.
3D left ventricular stroke, end-diastolic, and end-systolic volumes showed significant improvement trends.
MLCL/CL ratio improved, correlating with clinical outcomes; injection-site reactions were the main adverse event.
entries.limitations: Only 10 patients enrolled with 8 completing week 168, severely limiting statistical power and generalizability.
entries.context: TAZPOWER is the only completed RCT of elamipretide (SS-31) in a human rare disease population, making this 168-week extension the longest controlled human dataset available for this mitochondria-targeting peptide.
entries.landmarkReason: Only completed RCT and longest human efficacy dataset for SS-31

entries.title: Thymosin beta 4 as an Alzheimer disease intervention target identified using human brain organoids.
entries.displayTitle: Thymosin Beta-4 Rescues Alzheimer Pathology in Brain Organoids and Mice
entries.studyType: In vitro mechanistic study — human brain organoids and mouse model
entries.summary: Using iPSC-derived cerebral organoids with familial AD mutations and 5xFAD mice, researchers found TMSB4X/Thymosin beta-4 expression is reduced in AD neurons; exogenous Tβ4 treatment rescued neurodevelopmental deficits and reduced amyloid-beta formation.
keyFindings: Mature neuron formation was markedly reduced in fAD organoids versus healthy controls.
TMSB4X (Tβ4) expression significantly decreased in fAD organoid neurons and AD patient excitatory neurons.
Tβ4 treatment rescued neurodevelopmental deficits and Aβ production in fAD organoids.
Beneficial neuroprotective effects of Tβ4 were confirmed in 5xFAD mouse model.
entries.limitations: Organoid and mouse models may not fully recapitulate sporadic human AD; no human clinical data provided.
entries.context: This is among the first studies to identify Thymosin beta-4 as a neuroprotective intervention target in Alzheimer's disease using human iPSC-derived organoids, establishing a mechanistic link between Tβ4 deficiency and AD pathology.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing Tβ4's neuroprotective role in AD pathology

entries.title: Plasmacytoid dendritic cells alleviate allergic asthma via airway epithelial cell-dependent thymosin β4 expression.
entries.displayTitle: Thymosin β4 From Lung pDCs Suppresses Allergic Asthma Inflammation
entries.journal: The Journal of allergy and clinical immunology
entries.studyType: Animal model — mouse (with translational human data)
entries.summary: This study investigated the role of plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDCs) and thymosin β4 (Tβ4) in a mouse model of house dust mite-induced allergic asthma, finding that pDC-derived Tβ4 suppresses inflammatory monocyte recruitment by inhibiting JAK1/STAT6/CCL2 signaling in alveolar macrophages.
keyFindings: pDC depletion exacerbated T2-mediated asthma phenotypes via CCR2-dependent inflammatory monocyte accumulation.
Tβ4 was among the most upregulated genes in asthmatic lung pDCs, driven by epithelial IL-33.
Tβ4 supplementation reversed asthma exacerbation by inhibiting JAK1/STAT6/EGR2-driven CCL2 in macrophages.
Serum Tβ4 levels were decreased in both asthmatic mice and humans, suggesting increased macrophage uptake.
entries.limitations: Mechanistic findings are primarily from mouse models; human data are limited to serum Tβ4 correlations without interventional evidence.
entries.context: This study establishes a novel mechanistic role for Tβ4 in allergic airway disease, identifying pDCs as a primary lung source and alveolar macrophages as the functional target, providing a potential therapeutic rationale for Tβ4 supplementation in asthma.
entries.landmarkReason: Foundational mechanistic study establishing Tβ4's anti-inflammatory role in allergic asthma

entries.title: A first-in-human, randomized, double-blind, single- and multiple-dose, phase I study of recombinant human thymosin β4 in healthy Chinese volunteers.
entries.displayTitle: TB-500 Safe and Well-Tolerated in First Human Phase I Trial
entries.journal: Journal of cellular and molecular medicine
entries.summary: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase I study evaluated recombinant human thymosin β4 (NL005) in 84 healthy Chinese volunteers across single- and multiple-dose cohorts. No dose-limiting toxicities or serious adverse events were observed, and PK parameters scaled dose-proportionally.
keyFindings: Seven single-dose cohorts tested 0.05–25.0 µg/kg IV; all adverse events were mild to moderate.
No dose-limiting toxicities or serious adverse events in any of the 84 subjects.
Cmax and AUC increased proportionally with dose across all cohorts.
No significant drug accumulation detected after 10 consecutive daily doses.
entries.limitations: Study limited to healthy volunteers in a single ethnic population; no efficacy endpoints were assessed.
entries.context: This is the first published randomized controlled human trial of recombinant thymosin β4, establishing foundational safety and PK data that support further clinical development, including trials for acute myocardial infarction.
entries.landmarkReason: First human RCT and pharmacokinetic/dose-finding study

entries.title: Effects of Tesamorelin on Neurocognitive Impairment in Persons With HIV and Abdominal Obesity.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin Reduces Waist Circumference But Not Cognitive Impairment in HIV
entries.summary: This 6-month Phase 2 open-label RCT in 73 virally suppressed HIV patients with abdominal obesity compared tesamorelin (2 mg/day SC) to standard of care on neurocognitive outcomes. Tesamorelin reduced waist circumference but failed to produce a significant between-group improvement in neurocognitive performance.
keyFindings: Tesamorelin group showed trend toward cognitive improvement (mean change 0.146) but between-group difference was non-significant (P = .673).
Tesamorelin significantly reduced waist circumference vs standard of care (median difference −2.7 cm; P = .015).
IGF-1 levels increased with tesamorelin but did not correlate with cognitive scores or waist circumference changes.
Study was underpowered and lacked a placebo arm, limiting conclusions about cognitive benefit.
entries.limitations: Open-label design without a placebo arm and insufficient statistical power limit the ability to draw definitive conclusions about cognitive effects.
entries.context: This is among the first RCTs to prospectively test whether tesamorelin's established metabolic benefits in HIV-associated lipodystrophy translate into neurocognitive improvement, a clinically important and understudied outcome in virally suppressed HIV patients.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 2 RCT — first trial testing tesamorelin's effect on neurocognition in HIV

entries.title: Efficacy and safety of tesamorelin in people with HIV on integrase inhibitors.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin Reduces Visceral and Hepatic Fat in HIV Patients on Integrase Inhibitors
entries.summary: A randomized double-blind trial of 38 HIV-positive adults on integrase inhibitor-based regimens tested tesamorelin 2 mg daily vs. placebo over 12 months. Tesamorelin significantly reduced visceral fat, hepatic fat fraction, and trunk-to-appendicular fat ratio with no worsening of glycemic control.
keyFindings: Visceral fat declined by median 25 cm² with tesamorelin vs. +14 cm² with placebo (P = 0.001).
Hepatic fat fraction fell 4.2% with tesamorelin vs. 0.5% with placebo (P = 0.01).
Trunk-to-appendicular fat ratio improved significantly with tesamorelin (P = 0.03).
Adverse event rates, including hyperglycemia, were similar between tesamorelin and placebo groups.
entries.limitations: Subgroup analysis of only 31 completers from a parent trial limits statistical power and generalizability.
entries.context: Tesamorelin's phase III trials predated integrase inhibitors, now the standard of HIV care. This is the first dedicated RCT evidence confirming tesamorelin's efficacy and safety specifically in patients on INSTI-based regimens, filling a critical clinical evidence gap.
entries.landmarkReason: First RCT evidence in INSTI-treated HIV patients

entries.title: Effects of tesamorelin on hepatic transcriptomic signatures in HIV-associated NAFLD.
entries.displayTitle: Tesamorelin Reduces Liver Fat and Modulates Hepatic Gene Pathways in HIV-NAFLD
entries.summary: Using paired liver biopsies from a randomized placebo-controlled trial, this study examined hepatic transcriptomic changes induced by tesamorelin in HIV-associated NAFLD over 1 year, revealing gene pathway shifts favoring reduced inflammation, improved oxidative phosphorylation, and better fibrosis-related gene scores.
keyFindings: Tesamorelin increased hepatic gene sets involved in oxidative phosphorylation versus placebo.
Decreased expression of gene sets contributing to inflammation, tissue repair, and cell division.
Reciprocally modulated hepatocellular carcinoma prognosis gene sets in a favorable direction.
Hepatic gene expression changes correlated with improved fibrosis-related gene scores.
entries.limitations: Substudy with a small biopsy cohort limits statistical power and generalizability beyond HIV-positive individuals with NAFLD.
entries.context: This is the first mechanistic study explaining how tesamorelin reduces liver fat and prevents fibrosis progression in HIV-associated NAFLD, making it foundational for understanding pulsatile GH action on the liver in this population.
entries.landmarkReason: RCT mechanistic substudy — first transcriptomic evidence for tesamorelin's hepatic effects

entries.title: Neoadjuvant immunochemotherapy plus thymalfasin in locally advanced gastric cancer: a prospective clinical trial.
entries.displayTitle: Thymalfasin Plus Immunochemotherapy Achieves 30% Pathological Complete Response in Gastric Cancer
entries.summary: A phase II trial evaluated neoadjuvant serplulimab, SOX chemotherapy, and thymalfasin in 30 patients with stage III gastric/gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma. The regimen achieved a 30% pathological complete response rate and 56.7% major pathological response with an acceptable safety profile.
keyFindings: Pathological complete response (pCR) achieved in 30.0% (9/30) of patients.
Major pathological response (MPR) achieved in 56.7% (17/30) of patients.
N-stage downstaging observed in 80.0% (24/30); ypN0 status in 63.3% (19/30).
Grade ≥3 adverse events in 26.7%; only one relapse and no deaths at 14-month median follow-up.
entries.limitations: Single-arm design with only 30 patients and short follow-up of 14 months; no control arm to isolate thymalfasin's specific contribution.
entries.context: This is among the first prospective trials to evaluate thymalfasin combined with anti-PD-1 immunochemotherapy as neoadjuvant therapy, providing early efficacy, safety, and immune-profiling data to guide future randomized trials in gastric cancer.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 2 clinical trial — primary human efficacy and safety evidence
