entries.title: The efficacy and safety of thymosin α1 for sepsis (TESTS): multicentre, double blinded, randomised, placebo controlled, phase 3 trial.
entries.displayTitle: Phase 3 RCT: Thymosin α1 Does Not Reduce Sepsis Mortality
entries.summary: This multicentre, double-blind, phase 3 RCT enrolled 1,089 adults with sepsis across 22 Chinese centres, randomising them to subcutaneous thymosin α1 or placebo for seven days. No significant difference in 28-day all-cause mortality was observed between groups (23.4% vs 24.1%).
keyFindings: 28-day mortality: 23.4% (thymosin α1) vs 24.1% (placebo); HR 0.99, 95% CI 0.77–1.27; P=0.93.
No secondary or safety outcomes differed significantly between groups.
Subgroup signal: patients ≥60 years showed HR 0.81 (0.61–1.09); diabetic patients HR 0.58 (0.35–0.99).
Younger patients (<60 years) showed higher mortality risk with thymosin α1 (HR 1.67, 1.04–2.67).
entries.limitations: Trial conducted exclusively in China, limiting generalisability; subgroup findings (age, diabetes) are hypothesis-generating and require independent replication.
entries.context: This is the largest and most rigorous randomised trial of thymosin α1 in sepsis, providing the definitive human evidence base for its immunomodulatory use in critical illness and setting the standard for future sepsis immunotherapy trials.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 trial — primary definitive evidence

entries.title: Comprehensive Review of the Safety and Efficacy of Thymosin Alpha 1 in Human Clinical Trials.
entries.displayTitle: Thymosin Alpha-1 Safe and Effective Across 11,000+ Human Subjects
entries.journal: Alternative therapies in health and medicine
entries.summary: A narrative systematic review synthesizing over 30 clinical trials involving more than 11,000 human subjects evaluated Thymosin Alpha-1's safety and efficacy across COVID-19, autoimmune conditions, and cancer. The review found consistent evidence of tolerability and immune-modulating effectiveness across all three indication categories.
keyFindings: Over 11,000 human subjects across 30+ trials showed consistent safety and tolerability of Thymosin Alpha-1.
Tα1 demonstrated significant effectiveness in treating COVID-19, autoimmune disorders, and cancer.
Authors conclude the 2023 FDA restriction on Tα1 compounding is unsupported by the clinical evidence base.
Tα1 is characterized as a well-tolerated immune modulator warranting continued prescription availability.
entries.limitations: Narrative rather than quantitative meta-analysis; susceptible to selection bias and lacks pooled effect-size estimates, limiting statistical rigor.
entries.context: This is the broadest human-evidence synthesis to date on Thymosin Alpha-1, covering the widest range of indications and the largest aggregate subject count, making it a key reference for understanding the overall clinical evidence profile of the peptide.
entries.landmarkReason: Systematic review synthesizing human evidence across 30+ trials and 11,000+ subjects

entries.title: Tirzepatide for Obesity Treatment and Diabetes Prevention.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide Cuts Diabetes Progression 93% in Obese Prediabetes Patients
entries.journal: The New England journal of medicine
entries.summary: The SURMOUNT-1 phase 3 RCT followed 1,032 participants with obesity and prediabetes for 176 weeks. Tirzepatide produced up to 19.7% weight loss and reduced progression to type 2 diabetes by 93% compared to placebo.
keyFindings: Weight loss at 176 weeks: −12.3%, −18.7%, and −19.7% for 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg vs. −1.3% placebo.
Type 2 diabetes incidence: 1.3% (tirzepatide) vs. 13.3% (placebo); hazard ratio 0.07 (P<0.001).
Diabetes protection persisted 17 weeks post-treatment: 2.4% vs. 13.7% incidence.
Adverse events were primarily mild-to-moderate gastrointestinal, concentrated in first 20 weeks.
entries.limitations: Participants were limited to those with obesity and prediabetes, limiting generalizability to broader populations or those with established type 2 diabetes.
entries.context: This 3-year extension of SURMOUNT-1 provides the longest controlled evidence for tirzepatide in obesity, demonstrating durable diabetes prevention at a scale and magnitude not previously shown for a GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 trial — primary long-term efficacy and safety evidence

entries.title: Continued Treatment With Tirzepatide for Maintenance of Weight Reduction in Adults With Obesity: The SURMOUNT-4 Randomized Clinical Trial.
entries.displayTitle: Continuing Tirzepatide Maintains 25% Weight Loss vs. Regain on Placebo
entries.summary: This phase 3 randomized withdrawal trial enrolled 670 adults with obesity through a 36-week open-label tirzepatide lead-in, then randomized them to continue tirzepatide or switch to placebo for 52 weeks. Continued tirzepatide produced a total 25.3% weight reduction versus 9.9% with placebo, while discontinuation caused substantial weight regain.
keyFindings: Lead-in phase achieved mean 20.9% weight reduction before randomization.
Tirzepatide group lost an additional 5.5% from week 36–88; placebo group regained 14.0%.
89.5% on tirzepatide maintained ≥80% of lead-in weight loss vs. only 16.6% on placebo.
Overall mean weight reduction week 0–88: 25.3% (tirzepatide) vs. 9.9% (placebo).
entries.limitations: Excludes patients with diabetes, limiting generalizability; withdrawal design cannot assess outcomes beyond 88 weeks.
entries.context: SURMOUNT-4 is the pivotal maintenance trial establishing that tirzepatide's weight-loss benefits require continued treatment, directly informing long-term prescribing guidelines and regulatory labeling.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 trial — primary maintenance evidence

entries.title: Tirzepatide Once Weekly for the Treatment of Obesity.
entries.displayTitle: Tirzepatide Achieves Up to 20.9% Weight Loss in Phase 3 Trial
entries.journal: The New England journal of medicine
entries.summary: The SURMOUNT-1 phase 3 RCT tested once-weekly tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) versus placebo in 2,539 adults with obesity over 72 weeks. All three doses produced substantial, sustained weight loss far exceeding placebo, with the 15 mg dose achieving a mean 20.9% reduction in body weight.
keyFindings: Mean weight loss at 72 weeks: -15.0%, -19.5%, and -20.9% for 5, 10, and 15 mg doses vs. -3.1% for placebo.
91% of 15 mg participants achieved ≥5% weight reduction vs. 35% with placebo.
57% of 15 mg participants achieved ≥20% weight reduction vs. only 3% with placebo.
Most adverse events were mild-to-moderate gastrointestinal effects occurring during dose escalation.
entries.limitations: Trial excluded participants with type 2 diabetes, limiting generalizability to that large population with obesity.
entries.context: SURMOUNT-1 is the pivotal phase 3 trial establishing tirzepatide's efficacy for obesity, demonstrating weight loss exceeding any previously approved anti-obesity pharmacotherapy and supporting regulatory approval.
entries.landmarkReason: Phase 3 trial — primary evidence for obesity indication
